past_medical_history: Gestational hypertension during recent pregnancy, resolved postpartum
Iron deficiency anemia diagnosed during pregnancy, treated with oral ferrous sulfate
Uncomplicated lower-segment cesarean section 10 days prior for fetal distress
Mild postpartum depression, not yet on pharmacotherapy
No prior history of seizures, syncope, or cardiac disease
family_history: Mother has hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Father died at age 44 from 'heart problems' — family reports he collapsed at work and could not be resuscitated
Paternal grandmother reportedly died suddenly in her sleep at age 51
One younger sister, age 24, healthy but reports occasional palpitations
No known family history of epilepsy or neurological disease

social_history.alcohol: None — abstinent during pregnancy and postpartum
social_history.occupation: Software engineer, currently on maternity leave
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and newborn in a single-story apartment
social_history.exercise: Previously active — regular yoga and walking. Currently limited activity postpartum post-cesarean

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody. No dysarthria. Attention intact — serial 7s performed without error. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No aphasia. No post-ictal confusion at the time of evaluation, approximately 40 minutes after the most recent event. Mildly anxious but cooperative.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 3mm to 2mm. Fundoscopy: no papilledema, no hemorrhages. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact in all divisions. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally. No pronator drift. Mild tenderness at cesarean incision site on abdominal palpation, but no focal motor deficits.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all four extremities. No sensory level. No dermatomal loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus. No hyperreflexia.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. Gait slightly guarded due to postoperative abdominal discomfort.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or oral trauma. No evidence of head injury. Husband reports patient's eyes 'rolled back' during the episode and she appeared pale and diaphoretic immediately before collapsing. Mild bilateral lower extremity edema, consistent with recent pregnancy. Well-healed cesarean incision without signs of infection.

patient.chief_complaint: Two episodes of sudden loss of consciousness with witnessed jerking movements, 10 days postpartum, referred as 'postpartum seizures — rule out eclampsia'
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Priya Sharma is a 29-year-old woman, 10 days post-cesarean section, who presents to the emergency department via ambulance after two episodes of sudden loss of consciousness with witnessed convulsive movements over the past 6 hours. The first episode occurred at approximately 10:00 AM while she was standing in the kitchen preparing a bottle for her newborn. Her husband, who was in the adjacent room, heard a crash and found her on the floor, unconscious, with her arms and legs stiffening and then jerking rhythmically for approximately 10-15 seconds. He describes her face as 'ghostly pale' and notes she was drenched in sweat just before the fall. She regained consciousness within approximately 20-30 seconds and was oriented within 1-2 minutes, though she felt lightheaded and nauseated. She had no recollection of the fall itself. She denied any preceding aura, visual disturbance, headache, or epigastric pain. She rested on the couch and felt well enough within 15 minutes to resume caring for the baby. The second episode occurred at approximately 3:30 PM while she was climbing the stairs to retrieve diapers from a closet. Her husband witnessed her suddenly stop mid-step, turn pale, and collapse backward. He caught her partially, preventing a significant fall. She again had brief jerking of all four limbs lasting approximately 8-10 seconds and was unresponsive during the episode. She recovered consciousness within 30 seconds and again returned to baseline rapidly. EMS was called and she was transported to the referring hospital where she was evaluated for suspected eclampsia given her recent pregnancy complicated by gestational hypertension. At the outside facility, blood pressure was 124/78 mmHg, she was afebrile, and a non-contrast CT of the head was unremarkable. Magnesium sulfate was initiated empirically and she was transferred to our tertiary center for further evaluation. Her husband emphasizes that both episodes seemed to occur during physical exertion — standing for a prolonged period and climbing stairs — and that she appeared very pale immediately before each collapse. She reports having felt occasional brief episodes of her 'heart racing' over the past two weeks but attributed them to anxiety about the new baby and postpartum hormonal changes. She denies headache, visual changes, right upper quadrant pain, or peripheral edema beyond what she considers normal for her postpartum state.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, mildly increased likely secondary to anxiety
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves and sleep spindles seen in drowsiness; no epileptiform discharges in any state

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric bilateral slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline within 45 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Patient reported feeling mildly lightheaded during hyperventilation.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Eclamptic seizures would also typically be expected to produce cortical dysfunction. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute study. A single normal interictal EEG does not definitively exclude epilepsy, though sensitivity is approximately 50%. No events were captured during the recording.

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 82 bpm
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (2 PVCs captured in 10-second tracing)
PVCs with left bundle branch block morphology and inferior axis, suggestive of right ventricular outflow tract origin
QTc 452 ms — borderline, at the upper limit of normal; may be influenced by recent pregnancy and postpartum state
No ST-segment elevation or depression
No Brugada pattern identified
Subtle T-wave inversions in leads V1-V3 — could represent a normal female repolarization variant or, in the appropriate context, right ventricular pathology
No epsilon waves definitively identified, though small notching at terminal QRS in V1 is noted and may warrant further evaluation
No delta waves or short PR to suggest accessory pathway
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs of RVOT morphology. Subtle T-wave inversions in V1-V3 and possible terminal QRS notching in V1 are noted. No definitive arrhythmia captured on this resting 12-lead ECG.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 31.4 % (L), MCV 76.3 fL (L), Magnesium 2.8 mg/dL (H), Protein trace  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Urine protein trace — likely residual gestational

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with intermittent nonsustained ventricular tachycardia
output.axis: Normal axis during sinus rhythm; left superior axis during VT runs
findings: Baseline: Normal sinus rhythm, average rate 78 bpm (range 62-112)
Frequent PVCs: 4,832 PVCs in 24 hours (approximately 5.6% of total beats), predominantly monomorphic with LBBB morphology and inferior axis (RVOT origin)
Nonsustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT): 7 runs captured, longest run 12 beats at rate 185 bpm
2 runs of NSVT correlated temporally with patient diary entries reporting 'heart racing' and lightheadedness while breastfeeding in seated position
One run of NSVT occurred during sleep (3:22 AM) — rate 170 bpm, 8 beats, patient remained asleep
No sustained VT captured during monitoring period
No supraventricular tachycardia
No significant bradycardia or pauses
No AV conduction abnormalities
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitoring reveals a significant burden of PVCs (5.6%) with monomorphic RVOT morphology and 7 runs of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia, the longest being 12 beats at 185 bpm. Two episodes of NSVT correlated with patient-reported symptoms of palpitations and lightheadedness. Sustained VT with hemodynamic collapse is the likely mechanism of the patient's syncopal episodes.

Echocardiogram.value: Focal hypokinesis of RV free wall, subtricuspid region
No significant valvular stenosis or regurgitation (aside from mild TR)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Right ventricular size Mildly dilated  (H), RV free wall motion Focal hypokinesis of RV free wall, subtricuspid region  (H), Tricuspid regurgitation Mild  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Mildly dilated right ventricle (basal diameter 4.3 cm)
Focal hypokinesis of RV free wall, subtricuspid region

findings.location: Right ventricular free wall — subtricuspid region and RV outflow tract

signal_characteristics.T1_mapping: Elevated native T1 values in RV free wall (1180 ms, normal <1050 ms)
signal_characteristics.Late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall and subepicardial enhancement in RV free wall, subtricuspid region

findings.location: Right ventricular free wall, most prominent subtricuspid and extending toward RVOT

signal_characteristics.T1_weighted: High signal intensity consistent with fatty infiltration on T1-weighted images
signal_characteristics.fat_suppression: Signal attenuation on fat-suppressed sequences confirming fatty component

findings.size: RV end-diastolic volume index 112 mL/m² (normal <97 mL/m² for females)

additional_observations: Regional akinesia of subtricuspid RV free wall and dyskinesia of RVOT on cine imaging
No left ventricular involvement at this time
output.impression: Combined with ECG findings (T-wave inversions V1-V3, PVCs with LBBB morphology) and arrhythmia burden on Holter, the diagnosis of ARVC is established. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: eclampsia vs cardiac syncope postpartum differential diagnosis

results.title: Cardiac Causes of Syncope Mimicking Seizures in the Peripartum Period
results.source: Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 2021
results.summary: Case series of 14 women presenting with presumed eclamptic seizures who were ultimately diagnosed with cardiac arrhythmias. Key distinguishing features: rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, exertional triggers, normal blood pressure at time of events, and absence of proteinuria or laboratory evidence of preeclampsia.

results.title: Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy Unmasked During Pregnancy: Systematic Review
results.summary: Systematic review of 32 cases of ARVC presenting during or shortly after pregnancy. The hemodynamic changes of pregnancy (increased blood volume, increased heart rate) can unmask previously subclinical ARVC. Ventricular arrhythmias may first present peripartum. Family history of sudden cardiac death was present in 65% of cases. Presentation as 'seizures' led to initial misdiagnosis in 40% of cases.

results.title: Distinguishing Convulsive Syncope from Epileptic Seizures
results.summary: Convulsive syncope occurs in up to 12% of all syncope episodes. Myoclonic jerks during syncope result from cerebral hypoperfusion and can closely mimic tonic-clonic seizures. Key differentiating features: duration typically <15 seconds, rapid recovery (<2 minutes), pallor before event, and no post-ictal confusion.

output.summary: The literature supports that cardiac syncope, particularly from ventricular arrhythmias, can closely mimic seizures and is frequently misdiagnosed as epilepsy or eclampsia in the peripartum period. ARVC may be unmasked during pregnancy due to hemodynamic stress. Key distinguishing features from eclampsia include: normal blood pressure at time of events, absence of proteinuria/laboratory evidence of preeclampsia, exertional triggers, brief convulsive movements (<15 seconds), rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, and family history of sudden cardiac death.

interactions: Magnesium sulfate may potentiate neuromuscular blockade if patient requires intubation — use caution with non-depolarizing agents
Magnesium sulfate may enhance hypotensive effect of calcium channel blockers (not currently prescribed but relevant if initiated)
contraindications: In a patient with suspected cardiac arrhythmia as the cause of syncope, magnesium sulfate is not contraindicated but is unlikely to be therapeutic and may mask arrhythmia on monitoring by suppressing PVCs
warnings: Current serum magnesium is 2.8 mg/dL (elevated) — continued infusion may lead to hypermagnesemia
Monitor deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output during infusion
If eclampsia is excluded, discontinuation of magnesium sulfate should be considered
alternatives: If antiarrhythmic therapy needed: beta-blockers (sotalol, nadolol) are first-line for ARVC-associated arrhythmias
If seizure prophylaxis truly needed: levetiracetam is preferred over magnesium for non-eclamptic seizures

output.query: ARVC diagnostic criteria 2010 Task Force revised criteria

results.title: Diagnosis of Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy/Dysplasia: Proposed Modification of the Task Force Criteria
results.summary: The 2010 Revised Task Force Criteria for ARVC diagnosis require combinations of major and minor criteria across 6 categories: (1) Global/regional RV dysfunction and structural alterations, (2) Tissue characterization of RV wall, (3) Repolarization abnormalities, (4) Depolarization/conduction abnormalities, (5) Arrhythmias, (6) Family history. Definite diagnosis requires 2 major, or 1 major + 2 minor, or 4 minor criteria from different categories.

results.title: 2023 ESC Guidelines for Management of Cardiomyopathies
results.summary: Updated guidance on ARVC management emphasizes early genetic testing, family screening, exercise restriction, and risk stratification for sudden cardiac death. ICD implantation is recommended for patients with sustained VT, unexplained syncope, or significant RV dysfunction. Beta-blockers are first-line antiarrhythmic therapy.

output.summary: ARVC is diagnosed using the 2010 Revised Task Force Criteria, which combine structural, histological, ECG, arrhythmia, and family history parameters. This patient meets criteria across multiple categories: major structural criterion (CMR showing regional RV akinesia/dyskinesia with RV dilation or reduced RVEF), minor repolarization criterion (T-wave inversions V1-V3), major arrhythmia criterion (NSVT with LBBB morphology and superior axis), and possible family history criterion (sudden cardiac death in first-degree relative <35 years if confirmed). This would satisfy requirements for definite ARVC diagnosis.

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm progressing to nonsustained ventricular tachycardia at peak exercise
output.axis: Normal at rest; superior axis during VT
findings: Resting: Normal sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with occasional PVCs
Stage 2 (Bruce protocol): PVC burden increased significantly, couplets noted
Stage 3: At peak heart rate 156 bpm, 2 runs of nonsustained VT (8 and 14 beats) with LBBB morphology and superior axis
Patient reported dizziness during the second VT run — test terminated per protocol
Recovery: VT resolved within 2 minutes of exercise cessation, sinus rhythm restored
No ST-segment changes suggestive of ischemia
Blood pressure response appropriate during exercise (peak 148/82 mmHg)
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test is markedly positive for exercise-induced ventricular tachycardia. The patient became symptomatic (dizzy) during VT, confirming that hemodynamically significant VT is the likely mechanism of her syncopal episodes.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope secondary to arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal blood pressure at presentation, normal liver function, normal platelets, normal LDH, minimal proteinuria, rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, no headache or visual changes
Normal EEG without epileptiform discharges, very brief convulsive movements (<15 seconds), rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, exertional triggers, pallor before episodes
Episodes triggered by exertion rather than typical vasovagal triggers (pain, prolonged standing, emotional stress), negative tilt table test, family history of sudden cardiac death
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL is mild anemia, unlikely to cause syncope at this level, does not explain convulsive features or family history
Normal LV ejection fraction on echocardiography, normal BNP, no signs of heart failure; the pathology is right ventricular, not left ventricular

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with PVCs, subtle T-wave inversions V1-V3, borderline QTc

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG — no epileptiform abnormalities, supporting non-epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, liver function, cardiac markers, coagulation, and urinalysis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild iron deficiency anemia, elevated magnesium (exogenous), normal liver/renal/cardiac markers — excludes eclampsia and HELLP

optimal_actions.action: Request continuous cardiac monitoring with Holter monitor given exertional syncope and normal resting ECG
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frequent PVCs with RVOT morphology and multiple runs of nonsustained VT

optimal_actions.action: Request transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly dilated RV with focal hypokinesis of subtricuspid free wall, normal LV function

optimal_actions.action: Request cardiac MRI for detailed structural and tissue characterization of right ventricle
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Fibrofatty infiltration, regional RV akinesia/dyskinesia, RV dilation, reduced RVEF — meets major ARVC criteria

optimal_actions.action: Search literature for ARVC diagnostic criteria and eclampsia vs cardiac syncope differential
optimal_actions.expected_finding: 2010 Task Force Criteria for ARVC, evidence supporting cardiac etiology over eclampsia

optimal_actions.action: Recommend genetic testing for desmosomal gene mutations and first-degree family screening
Recommend electrophysiology consultation for risk stratification and ICD consideration

critical_actions: Recognize that the presentation is more consistent with cardiac syncope than eclampsia or epilepsy based on rapid recovery, exertional triggers, pallor, and family history
Order continuous cardiac monitoring (Holter) to capture paroxysmal arrhythmia not seen on resting ECG
Obtain cardiac imaging (echocardiography and/or cardiac MRI) to evaluate for structural right ventricular disease
Discontinue empiric magnesium sulfate and antiepileptic therapy once eclampsia is excluded
Recommend exercise restriction pending cardiac evaluation
Refer to electrophysiology for ICD risk stratification given syncope with ARVC
contraindicated_actions: Initiating or continuing antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam) without evidence of epilepsy
Attributing the episodes to eclampsia and treating with magnesium sulfate long-term when labs do not support preeclampsia
Performing lumbar puncture — no clinical indication and delays appropriate cardiac workup
Allowing the patient to engage in strenuous physical activity before cardiac evaluation is complete
Prescribing Class IC antiarrhythmic agents (flecainide, propafenone) which are contraindicated in structural heart disease
Dismissing the family history of sudden cardiac death as coincidental
key_reasoning_points: Convulsive syncope (myoclonic jerks during syncope) can closely mimic tonic-clonic seizures and frequently leads to misdiagnosis as epilepsy or eclampsia

The brief duration of convulsive movements (<15 seconds), rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion (<2 minutes to baseline), and pallor before the event are hallmarks of syncope rather than seizure
Exertional triggers for loss of consciousness should always prompt cardiac evaluation, even in a young postpartum woman
Normal resting ECG does not exclude paroxysmal arrhythmia — continuous monitoring is essential when clinical suspicion is high
ARVC can be unmasked during or shortly after pregnancy due to increased hemodynamic stress, and the peripartum period is a recognized vulnerable window
Family history of sudden cardiac death (father at age 44, paternal grandmother at age 51) is a critical red flag that must not be overlooked
Normal blood pressure, normal liver function, normal platelets, and normal LDH effectively exclude eclampsia and HELLP syndrome

The subtle ECG findings (T-wave inversions V1-V3, PVCs with RVOT morphology, borderline QTc) are individually nonspecific but collectively suggest right ventricular pathology when interpreted in the appropriate clinical context

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: A postpartum woman presents with convulsive episodes initially diagnosed as eclamptic seizures. Normal blood pressure, labs excluding preeclampsia/HELLP, and a normal EEG redirect attention to the cardiac system. The resting ECG shows only subtle, non-diagnostic findings. The critical diagnostic clue — nonsustained VT with RVOT morphology — is buried in follow-up Holter monitoring, and the definitive diagnosis of ARVC requires cardiac MRI. Family history of sudden cardiac death, easily dismissed in the acute postpartum setting, is a pivotal clue.
